Folklore and Bank Loan Spreads

Christophe Godlewski & Laurent Weill

EM Strasbourg Business School, University of Strasbourg

International Review of Financial Analysis, 111 (2026), 105100

Presentation Overview

  • 1Motivation & Research Question
  • 2Conceptual Framework: Folklore as Measured Culture
  • 3Data & Methodology
  • 4Main Results
  • 5Additional Estimations & Heterogeneity
  • 6Robustness Checks
  • 7Conclusion & Implications

1. Motivation & Research Question

The Core Puzzle

Loan spreads reflect comprehensive risk assessment by lenders — yet considerable variation remains unexplained by standard determinants (legal framework, macro environment, borrower characteristics).

Research Gap: Cultural factors (trust, language, religion) have been shown to influence loan spreads, but folklore — as a deep-rooted cultural narrative — remains unexplored in financial contracting.

Central Hypothesis

The way a culture narrates and interprets risk through folklore influences contemporary credit risk perception and loan pricing.

Key Mechanism: Folklore operates as soft information — a cultural lens through which lenders interpret uncertainty, shaped by behavioral biases (loss aversion, negativity bias).

1.1 Theoretical Channels

Soft Information in Banking

Credit assessment involves subjective, non-verifiable inputs (Liberti & Petersen, 2011). In syndicated lending, lead arrangers structure deals and set spreads based on:

  • Hard signals: credit ratings, financial ratios
  • Soft signals: relationship quality, reputation, cultural priors
Our contribution: Folklore as a latent layer of soft information — shaping risk perception heuristics.

Behavioral Finance Lens

Well-documented biases amplify cultural narratives:

  • Loss aversion (Tversky & Kahneman, 1991): Individuals overweight potential losses relative to gains
  • Negativity bias (Baumeister et al., 2001): Greater attention to negative than positive stimuli
Prediction: Cultures with folklore emphasizing failed risk-taking will exhibit higher loan spreads — lenders respond more strongly to cautionary tales than heroic ones.

2. Conceptual Framework: Folklore as Measured Culture

What is Folklore?

Following Berezkin (2015): "All kinds of traditional stories and tales, long and short, sacred and profane" — found across 958 cultural societies from 199 countries.

Motif: A recurrent element (plot, character, symbolic image) appearing in at least two different traditions — the unit of quantitative analysis.

2,564 motifs catalogued; median appears in 18 traditions; median group contains 62 motifs.

Endurance & Transmission

Despite early 20th-century sourcing, motifs reflect deeply rooted values:

  • Oral traditions date back centuries or millennia (Silva & Tehrani, 2016; Vansina, 1985)
  • Persist among second-generation immigrants (Michalopoulos & Xue, 2021)
  • Shape contemporary economic attitudes: trust, gender norms, risk-taking
"Images and episodes in folklore appear to endure and possibly still shape how individuals perceive the world today."
— Michalopoulos & Xue (2021)

2.1 Classifying Risk-Related Motifs

Michalopoulos & Xue (2021) classify motifs using ConceptNet semantic network + Amazon Mechanical Turk human validation (9 coders per motif):

Successful Outcome
Motif K27g: Hero bathes in boiling water, emerges unharmed; antagonist perishes.
→ Celebrates resilience, hidden strength
Unsuccessful Outcome
Motif K27l1: Character freezes himself into ice, becomes trapped, unable to escape.
→ Cautionary tale of overconfidence, irreversible failure
Unclear / N/A
Outcome cannot be definitively classified, or motif is unrelated to challenge/competition despite semantic tagging.
Validation: Folklore variables predict cross-country differences in GPS risk-taking scores, new business registrations, and patents per capita — confirming economic relevance.

2.2 Folklore Varies Across Berezkin Groups

Figure 2 from the paper: average share of challenge-related motifs by Berezkin folklore group and outcome type.

Why this figure helps

The folklore measure is not country folklore in the abstract: it is a group-level distribution of story motifs linked to the lead lender's location.

Substantive variation

Challenge motifs range from below 5% in some groups to nearly 15% in others, with different mixes of success and failure.

Economic interpretation

The paper asks whether lenders exposed to more cautionary challenge stories price the same credit risk more conservatively.

2.3 Six Folklore Variables

Variable Definition Sample Mean
Challenge Share of all motifs related to challenge/competition 5.04%
Successful Share with positive outcome in challenge/competition 3.53%
Not successful Share with negative outcome in challenge/competition 0.94%
Unclear Share with unclassifiable outcome 1.64%
N/A Share semantically tagged but human-rejected 0.20%
Relatively Unsuccessful Not successful − Successful (our adapted measure) −2.59%

Note: We adapt Michalopoulos & Xue's definition by excluding "Unclear" from the synthetic measure, sharpening the contrast between positive and negative outcomes for credit risk asymmetry.

3. Data & Methodology

Loan Data (Bloomberg)

  • Sample: All syndicated loans to European borrowers, Jan 1999 – Dec 2017
  • Exclusions: Financial industry firms, public sector
  • Loan characteristics: Spread (bps), maturity, collateral, covenants, amount, currency, type, purpose
  • Lender data: Identity, nationality, headquarters, role, syndicate size
  • Lead lender identified via "Loan Agent" variable

Folklore Mapping

  • Link lead lender headquarters city to nearest Berezkin folklore group centroid
  • Geographical distance matching for subnational granularity
  • 33 unique Berezkin groups represented among lead lenders
Why lead lender? Structures the deal, underwrites risk, sets the baseline spread. Pricing primarily reflects lead lender's credit risk perception.

3.1 Sample Composition

1,671 Unique Loans
1,192 Unique Firms
316 Lead Banks

Borrower Countries (Top 5)

Country# Loans# Firms
Spain326241
France310214
Germany210151
Netherlands159115
Italy145107

Top 5 account for 69% of loans

Loan Characteristics (Mean)

VariableMean
Loan spread218 bps
Maturity6.1 years
Secured43%
Covenants9%
Syndicate size12 lenders
League table top 312%
Prior relationship11%

3.2 Empirical Model

We estimate OLS regressions with standard errors clustered at the lead lender folklore group level:

ln(Spreadi) = α + β · Folklorej + δ · Controlsi,j,k,l,m + εi

Indices

  • i = loan
  • j = lead bank (folklore group)
  • k = firm
  • l = region (NUTS-2)
  • m = country

Three Specifications

  • (1) Loan controls + Bank controls + Year dummies
  • (2) + Firm controls + Industry dummies
  • (3) + Country dummies (full specification)

3.3 Control Variables

Loan Level

  • Maturity (years)
  • Secured (dummy)
  • Amount (log USD millions)
  • Covenants (dummy)
  • Syndicate size
  • League table top 3
  • Prior relationship
  • Currency, type, purpose dummies
  • Year dummies

Bank Level

  • Bank size (log total assets)
  • Bank ROA
  • Bank capitalization (equity/assets)

Firm Level

  • Firm rating (dummy)
  • Firm size (log assets)
  • Firm leverage
  • Firm ROA
  • Firm current ratio
  • Industry dummies

Region & Country

  • GDP per capita (log, NUTS-2)
  • Private credit / GDP
  • Stock market cap / GDP
  • Rule of Law index
  • German legal origin
  • English legal origin

4. Main Results

We test three folklore specifications across progressively richer control sets:

Specification I: Challenge

Hypothesis: Overall risk-taking narratives elevate perceived uncertainty.

Finding: Challenge is positive & significant in specs (2) and (3). More challenge narratives are associated with higher spreads.

Specification II: Components

Hypothesis: Outcome matters — failure vs. success drives asymmetric response.

Finding: Not successful ↑ (significant); Successful = 0 (insignificant). Pattern is consistent with loss aversion.

Specification III: Relatively Unsuccessful

Hypothesis: Excess of failure over success is the key driver.

Finding: Positive & significant in ALL specifications (1%, 5%, 1% levels). Robust predictor.

Economic Magnitude

One-SD increase in Relatively Unsuccessful:

+22.86 bps → +10.5% increase relative to mean spread (218 bps)

Robust across all specs: Dominance of failure over success in challenge-related folklore consistently predicts higher loan spreads.

5. Heterogeneity: Bank & Firm Level

Does folklore matter uniformly? We split samples at medians of bank and firm characteristics.

Bank-Level Hypotheses

Fragility hypothesis: Weak banks (small, low ROA, low capital) are more influenced by folklore-driven risk perceptions.

Capacity hypothesis (alternative): Strong banks (large, profitable, well-capitalized) have greater cognitive resources to process soft information — including cultural cues.

Firm-Level Hypotheses

Fragility hypothesis: Weak firms (small, high leverage, low ROA) face amplified folklore effects — lenders are more sensitive to risk signals.

Capacity hypothesis (alternative): Strong firms have less differentiation from hard data, leaving more room for soft information (folklore) to influence pricing.

5.1 Bank Heterogeneity (Table 6)

Panel A: Above Median Bank Size Bank ROA Bank Cap. Panel B: Below Median Bank Size Bank ROA Bank Cap.
Challenge 2.869 2.217 5.503*** Challenge 4.996 9.044*** −10.726
Not successful 16.223** 16.004** 1.607 Not successful 3.933 5.524 22.941**
Rel. Unsuccessful 9.342** 8.443** 6.061** Rel. Unsuccessful 5.436 1.697 22.307***
# loans 264 248 234 # loans 266 281 296
Adj. R² 0.62 0.65 0.65 Adj. R² 0.68 0.62 0.62
Result: The capacity hypothesis is supported. Not successful and Relatively Unsuccessful are significant for larger, more profitable banks — suggesting sophisticated soft information processing. For capitalization, effects are significant on both sides, with stronger coefficients for low-capital banks.

5.2 Firm Heterogeneity (Table 7)

Panel A: Above Median Firm Size Firm Lev. Firm ROA Panel B: Below Median Firm Size Firm Lev. Firm ROA
Challenge 2.801 2.199 2.509 Challenge 4.482 4.680 6.707***
Not successful 11.984*** 4.411 8.755* Not successful 4.811 11.137* 5.017
Rel. Unsuccessful 9.192*** 1.931 7.118 Rel. Unsuccessful 4.873 8.434** 7.542***
# loans 281 262 264 # loans 248 268 265
Adj. R² 0.64 0.68 0.64 Adj. R² 0.61 0.62 0.64
Result: Again, the capacity hypothesis dominates. Not successful and Relatively Unsuccessful are significant for larger, more profitable, less indebted firms — where hard information offers less differentiation, leaving room for cultural narratives to shape pricing.

6. Robustness Checks

Three complementary strategies to ensure causal interpretation:

6.1 Control for Culture

Isolate folklore from other cultural dimensions: Hofstede dimensions, religion, trust, corruption.

6.2 Endogeneity: IV

Spatial instrumental variable: neighboring folklore within 225km radius as instrument.

6.3 Fixed Effects & Regional Controls

Country×year, lender country, borrower country, regional development, education, density.

6.1 Controlling for Alternative Culture (Table 8)

Adding Hofstede, religion, trust, corruption — folklore effects persist:

Hofstede
(1)(5)(9)		 Religion
(2)(6)(10)		 Trust
(3)(7)(11)		 Corruption
(4)(8)(12)
Challenge 4.294** 4.577** 4.481** 4.215*
Not successful 9.178* 8.804* 7.892 7.931*
Rel. Unsuccessful 10.296*** 8.710*** 8.925*** 8.361***
Successful −9.224** −4.390 −4.664 −4.014
Adj. R² 0.61 0.61 0.61 0.61–0.62
# loans 529 529 529 529

New finding: With Hofstede controls, Successful becomes significantly negative — heroic tales reduce spreads, reinforcing the asymmetry.

6.2 Instrumental Variables (Table 9)

Strategy: Neighboring folklore (225km radius) as instrument — captures cultural diffusion, exogenous to current financial decisions.

First Stage

Rel. Unsuccessfulj = γ0 + γ1 · Rel. Unsuccessful IVj + νj

Coefficient: 0.978*** (0.035)
F-statistic: 803.49 (>800)

Instrument is highly relevant — no weak instrument concern.

Second Stage

ln(Spreadi) = α + β · Rel. Unsuccessfulj + δ · Controls + εi

Coefficient: 5.808*** (2.165)
Significance: 1% level

Causal interpretation validated: Exogenous cultural variation in neighboring folklore drives loan spreads.

6.3 Fixed Effects & Regional Controls

Table 10: Fixed Effects

SpecificationRel. UnsuccessfulAdj. R²
Year + Industry7.151***0.63
+ Borrower country FE10.788***0.63
+ Lender country FE10.977***0.65
+ Borrower×Year FE9.393***0.69
+ Lender×Year FE12.368***0.68
Full FE set16.146***0.75

Coefficient increases with more demanding FE — not driven by country cycles or sector shocks.

Table 11: Regional Controls

Added ControlsRel. UnsuccessfulAdj. R²
Regional GDP pc8.990***0.64
+ Population density6.197***0.64
+ Tertiary education7.211***0.65

Folklore coefficient stable — not confounded by regional development, urbanization, or human capital.

7. Conclusion & Implications

Key Findings

  • Folklore affects loan spreads: Challenge-related motifs elevate perceived risk
  • Asymmetric response: Unsuccessful outcomes drive higher spreads; successful outcomes have no effect
  • Relatively Unsuccessful is a robust predictor — one SD increase → +10.5% higher spread
  • Heterogeneity: Effects stronger for stronger banks and firms — soft information complements hard data
  • Causal reading: IV, fixed effects, and regional controls support the interpretation

Implications

For Lenders & Risk Managers:
Cultural scripts in oral traditions explain cross-border spread variation not captured by standard risk models.
For Policymakers:
Culture-sensitive risk assessment frameworks may be warranted in multicultural lending contexts.
Broader Message:
"Not all data are digital, and not all signals are numerical. Sometimes, the stories of old still shape the price of risk — and the cost of capital."
